IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—cross‑lingual and low‑resource dependency parsing—using the Universal Dependencies (UD) collection and evaluation on 32 languages, which is directly relevant to the field. It provides evidence that UD harmonization materially improves multi‑source delexicalized transfer (reporting 70.1% vs. McDonald et al.'s 61.7%) and shows that a minimally supervised unsupervised parser with handcrafted POS‑based priors can outperform transfer parsers on many non‑Indo‑European (low‑resourced) languages. These findings are likely to change practitioner choices (UD as a better basis for transfer) and open follow‑on work on learning or designing universal priors for low‑resource languages, so the impact is strong though focused within parsing and cross‑lingual NLP.

CLARITY.justification: The manuscript has a recognizable section structure (Related Work, Data, Methods, Experiments, Conclusions) but the flow is disrupted by extensive duplicated and fragmented text (e.g. multiple repeated CoNLL/Universal Dependencies paragraphs and sentence fragments), which makes it hard to follow. Main contributions are stated only near the end rather than early in the introduction. Notation is introduced (pextstop, pextattach, λattach) but definitions and usage are scattered and not fully clarified, and many tables/figures are referenced but not present in the excerpt. Overall the writing contains frequent repetitions, formatting glitches and grammatical issues that require substantial editing for clarity.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper studies cross-lingual/delexicalized transfer and minimally-supervised unsupervised dependency parsing on Universal Dependencies v1.2 across 32 languages, a central NLP problem relevant to top venues. It uses standard experimental protocols (leave-one-out multi-source transfer), established parsers (Malt, MST, UDP), and common evaluation metrics (attachment scores), and compares against prior work (McDonald et al., Rosa), showing clear relevance to the parsing and low-resource/transfer learning community. The problem formulation, dataset choice, and positioning within related work are appropriate for a top-tier NLP/ML conference audience.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear claims (comparison of multi-source delexicalized transfer vs. a minimally supervised UDP enriched by pextstop/pextattach priors) and provides experimental results (e.g. transfer parser reaching 70.1% vs. McDonald et al. 61.7%, and averages vs. Rosa), but the provided text omits crucial methodological details and controls. Concerns: training data was arbitrarily truncated to 10,000 tokens per language (Section 4.1) and only the first treebank per language was used, which can bias results; the manual priors are only coarsely described (Section 4.2.1) with no ablation or learning-curve analyses; and no statistical significance testing or full experimental configuration (hyperparameters, exact baselines/tables) is reported, making some superiority claims (Section 5.2, Conclusions) insufficiently supported. Mathematical content is minimal and appears internally consistent, but the experimental evidence is incomplete to fully validate all claims.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper runs cross-lingual experiments on 32 UD treebanks with leave-one-out multi-source transfer and compares delexicalized (Malt, MST) and a minimally supervised unsupervised parser enhanced with manually assigned priors (Sections 3–4, mention of Tables 3–5), so the experimental breadth is reasonable. However, key methodological details and quantitative results are missing in the provided excerpt (exact parser hyperparameters, values for λattach/pextstop, full result tables, and training/evaluation protocols), and ablation/sensitivity analyses of the priors (beyond a brief “preliminary experiments showed less is more”) are not systematically reported. Related work is well covered, and the paper acknowledges some limitations (MST better on average; their method stronger on many non‑Indo‑European languages), but reproducibility and deeper error analysis are insufficiently detailed.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper largely applies and compares existing approaches (multi-source delexicalized transfer à la McDonald et al. (2011) and the unsupervised UDP of Mareček & Straka (2013) with external priors) on the Universal Dependencies collection, and reports empirical benefits (e.g. higher average transfer scores on UD and better performance of minimally supervised parser on non-Indo-European languages). Its main methodological novelty is limited to hand-crafted, language-independent attach/stop priors (Tables 3–4) and their use in UDP, plus learning priors from other treebanks — an incremental, application-focused extension rather than a genuinely new technique. The problem is well-studied and the paper differentiates its contributions empirically, but would be unsurprising to experts in cross-lingual/delexicalized parsing.

REPLICABILITY.justification: The paper provides several reproducible details (it uses Universal Dependencies v1.2, excludes Japan-KTC, uses only the first treebank per language, delexicalizes forms/lemmas/feats to underscores, uses leave-one-out multi-source transfer and takes the first 10,000 tokens per language resulting in ~300k tokens). It also names the exact tools used (Malt, MST, and the UDP implementation of Mareček and Straka (2013)) and states that some external priors were manually set (referenced as Table 3/4) and that the parser code was modified. However, many crucial details are missing or not shown in the provided text: specific hyperparameters and parser configuration choices are not given (“several parameter settings” but unspecified), the actual prior values and rule tables are only referenced (Table 3/4) but not included, training/inference settings for the UDP (iterations, random seeds, sampler settings) are not reported, and the authors note code changes without providing released code—making full reproduction only partially possible.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines (McDonald et al. 2011, Rosa 2015, MST/Malt parsers and classic unsupervised work) and reports direct numeric comparisons (e.g. McDonald 61.7% vs their 70.1%; Rosa 56.6% vs their 62.5%). It also acknowledges caveats about comparability due to differing annotation styles and states when prior methods perform better (MST better on average) and where their approach wins (non-Indo-European languages). However, some fairness issues remain (they subsample training data to 10k tokens per language and admit results are “not directly comparable”), and analysis of per-language/detailed error differences is limited, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.
